eli3: Traditional risk models (like Barra) use a fixed set of factors — market beta, size, value, industry — to decompose portfolio risk. ML risk models use machine learning to estimate factor exposures and covariance matrices more accurately, especially for non-linear relationships, tail risk, and dynamic correlations that change over time.

The key challenge in portfolio risk modelling: estimating the covariance matrix of N assets. With N = 500 stocks, the covariance matrix has 500×501/2 = 125,250 parameters to estimate from, say, 252 daily returns — there are more parameters than data points. This requires either a factor model (dimensionality reduction) or statistical regularisation. ML provides better regularisation and the ability to model non-linear, time-varying relationships.

Applications: better VaR estimates, improved portfolio optimisation (fewer estimation errors in the covariance matrix mean more stable optimal portfolios), dynamic factor models that adapt to regime changes, and tail risk models that go beyond normally-distributed return assumptions.

whenWhy.title: Covariance matrix estimation: from OLS to ML
whenWhy.body: OLS sample covariance: Σ̂ = (1/T) Σ (r_t - r̄)(r_t - r̄)ᵀ. With N = 500 stocks and T = 252 days: Σ̂ is rank 252 (singular); many eigenvalues are zero or near-zero. Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage: blend sample covariance with a structured target (identity matrix × average variance): Σ̂_LW = α × Σ̂_sample + (1-α) × Σ_target. Optimal α derived analytically to minimise expected loss. Random Matrix Theory (RMT): identify and remove eigenvalues consistent with pure noise (Marchenko-Pastur distribution); retain only eigenvalues carrying genuine information. ML approach (Deep Factor Model): train a deep network to predict next-period covariances using past return history and factor exposures; learns non-linear, time-varying factor structures. Advantage: adapts to regime changes; captures non-linear factor interactions. Disadvantage: requires large training sample; computationally expensive; black-box.

whenWhy.title: Factor risk models: Barra vs. ML
whenWhy.body: Commercial factor risk models (MSCI Barra, Axioma): decompose stock return variance into: systematic risk (from K factors: market, industries, style: value, momentum, size, quality, volatility) + idiosyncratic risk. Barra estimation: cross-sectional regression at each time t: r_i,t = Σ_k f_k,t × β_i,k + ε_i,t; factors f_k,t are estimated from the regression. Factor covariance matrix and idiosyncratic variance estimated from the time series of factor returns. ML improvements: (1) Auto-ML factor selection: instead of pre-specified factors, use elastic net or random forest to select from hundreds of candidate characteristics; factors may shift over time; (2) Factor exposure estimation: NN models for β̂_i,k as a non-linear function of company characteristics (size, growth, profitability); captures that the same characteristic has different risk implications in different market environments; (3) Idiosyncratic volatility forecasting: GARCH-based individual stock vol model; or ML model using historical vol, news, options-implied vol as inputs.

whenWhy.title: Tail risk and non-Gaussian risk models
whenWhy.body: Standard covariance-based risk models assume normal distributions — they systematically underestimate tail risk. Non-Gaussian ML approaches: (1) Filtered Historical Simulation (FHS): apply GARCH to scale historical returns to current volatility level, then bootstrap from the scaled historical distribution (captures fat tails and volatility clustering); (2) Extreme Value Theory (EVT): model the tail of the return distribution using a Generalized Pareto Distribution (GPD); more accurate for estimating probability of extreme events; (3) Copula models: model marginal distributions separately from dependence structure; t-copula captures tail dependence (joint extremes more likely than Gaussian); (4) VAE/GAN-based scenario generation: train a variational autoencoder or GAN on historical return scenarios; generate realistic stress scenarios including correlation spikes; (5) Neural network VaR: train a quantile regression NN directly on portfolio P&L using features (volatility measures, macro vars); outputs the conditional VaR without distributional assumptions.

deepDiveIntro: Barra performance attribution decomposition: r_p - r_b = (β_p - β_b) × f + α_p + ε_p, where r_p is portfolio return, r_b is benchmark, β_p-β_b is active factor exposure, f is factor return, α_p is pure alpha, ε_p is residual. Portfolio risk attribution: σ²_active = (β_active)ᵀ F Cov_factors F ᵀ(β_active) + Σ_i w²_i σ²_ε,i. First term = systematic active risk; second term = idiosyncratic active risk. A concentrated 10-stock portfolio has high idiosyncratic risk; a 200-stock factor-tilted portfolio has mainly systematic risk. Barra TE (tracking error) = √(σ²_active) / annualised; target TE 2-4% for active equity funds.

deepDive.heading: Dynamic Factor Models and Regime-Adaptive Risk
deepDive.body: Static factor risk models use a fixed factor structure estimated over a long history. In reality, correlations and factor exposures change over time. Dynamic approaches: (1) Rolling window estimation: re-estimate covariance matrix from the last T = 252 days of data; adapts to recent regime but discards older data; (2) Exponentially weighted covariance: Σ_t = λ × Σ_{t-1} + (1-λ) × r_t r_t ᵀ; EWMA model (λ = 0.94 in RiskMetrics); recent observations have more weight; (3) DCC-GARCH (Dynamic Conditional Correlation): model each stock's conditional volatility separately (GARCH) then model the correlation matrix dynamically; captures both changing variances and changing correlations; (4) Kalman filter factor model: factor exposures β_{i,k,t} evolve as random walk; Kalman filter tracks them dynamically; optimal state estimation as new returns arrive. ML extensions: (1) LSTM-based correlation forecasting: train on rolling covariance matrices; predict next period's covariance; (2) Transformer model: attention weights across time steps learn which historical periods are most relevant for current covariance estimation. [1]

deepDive.heading: Portfolio Optimisation with ML-Estimated Risk
deepDive.body: Better risk model → better portfolio optimisation. Mean-variance optimisation is notoriously sensitive to estimation error: a small error in expected returns or covariance matrix produces dramatically different optimal portfolios. ML risk model improvements: (1) More accurate covariance estimation → more stable optimal weights; fewer extreme positions; less turnover; (2) Robust optimisation: treat Σ as uncertain; find portfolio that maximises worst-case objective over the set of plausible Σ; (3) Denoised covariance (Lopez de Prado): apply RMT to remove noise eigenvalues; cleaner factor structure; portfolio weights are more stable; (4) Hierarchical Risk Parity (HRP): uses hierarchical clustering of the correlation matrix to construct equal-risk allocation without inverting the covariance matrix (matrix inversion amplifies estimation errors); (5) ML-corrected Black-Litterman: use ML models to generate prior views on expected returns; Black-Litterman framework blends ML views with equilibrium returns; confidence weights on ML views proportional to model accuracy. [2]

deepDive.heading: Credit Risk Modelling with ML
deepDive.body: Traditional credit risk: Merton structural model (firm's equity = call option on assets; default occurs when asset value < debt); Altman Z-score (linear discriminant model using 5 financial ratios). ML improvements: (1) Gradient boosted trees for PD (probability of default) estimation: trained on historical default events; 30-50 financial features; outperforms logistic regression in discriminatory power (AUC 0.85 vs. 0.78); (2) Neural networks for LGD (loss given default): complex non-linear relationship between collateral characteristics, economic conditions, and recovery rates; NN captures interactions linear models miss; (3) Survival analysis with ML: time-to-default modelling; handles censoring (loans that haven't yet defaulted); gradient boosted survival trees; (4) Network models: model the interconnected nature of credit risk in financial systems; graph neural networks for systemic risk; (5) NLP-based credit risk: extract information from loan documents, borrower financial statements (PDFs), covenant compliance — automate credit analyst work. Key regulatory constraint: Basel III requires interpretable credit models; ML models need explanation (SHAP) to satisfy regulatory requirements. [2]

takeaways: Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage: blend sample Σ with structured target; reduces estimation error for large covariance matrices. [1]
RMT: eigenvalues within Marchenko-Pastur distribution are noise; denoise by setting them to average eigenvalue. [1]
Barra risk decomposition: σ²_active = systematic active risk + idiosyncratic active risk; TE = √(σ²_active). [1]
DCC-GARCH: dynamic conditional correlations; captures regime changes in correlation structure. [1]
HRP (Hierarchical Risk Parity): cluster-based allocation without matrix inversion; avoids amplified estimation errors. [2]

edgeCases.q: How do you handle the 'curse of dimensionality' in covariance matrix estimation for large portfolios?
edgeCases.a: With N > T (more stocks than time observations), the sample covariance matrix is singular. Solutions by dimension: N = 100-500: Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage is sufficient; N = 500-2,000: factor model essential (reduce to 50-200 factors); pure LASSO/elastic net for sparse covariance estimation if the true structure is sparse; N > 2,000: hierarchical factor model (global factors → sector factors → stock-specific); sequential estimation avoids full N×N matrix. Practical choice: use a commercial factor risk model (Barra, Axioma) as the baseline (they've solved the estimation problem for standard use cases); add ML adjustments for non-linear factor exposures or dynamic correlations on top. Building a proprietary factor risk model from scratch for N > 500 stocks requires significant statistical expertise and data engineering — most asset managers use commercial models for this layer.

edgeCases.q: What is the impact of estimation error on portfolio optimisation vs. factor weighting?
edgeCases.a: The classic Markowitz optimisation problem: min w'Σw - λ w'μ. The solution is extremely sensitive to errors in μ̂ and Σ̂: Michaud (1989) showed that optimised portfolios often have worse out-of-sample performance than equal-weight portfolios because estimation errors are amplified by matrix inversion. Empirical finding: the covariance matrix errors are less damaging than expected return errors; μ̂ errors dominate. Practical implications: (1) Don't rely on estimated μ for optimisation; either use equal expected returns (Black-Litterman equilibrium) or factor model returns; (2) Use robust covariance (shrinkage + denoising) and constrain portfolio weights; (3) Resampled efficiency (Michaud 1998): run optimisation on bootstrap samples of returns; average the optimal weights; reduces error amplification; (4) Regularised Markowitz: add L1 (LASSO) or L2 (Ridge) penalty on weights; equivalent to imposing prior that weights are near zero; naturally creates sparse portfolios.

sources.label: Ledoit, O. & Wolf, M. (2004) — A Well-Conditioned Estimator for Large-Dimensional Covariance Matrices, JMVA
Lopez de Prado, M. — Machine Learning for Asset Managers, Cambridge University Press
